issue147-anthropic-cmt-final2-20260627: 'Uprear' maps the physical act of erecting a structure onto the founding of republican institutions, emphasizing deliberate human labor and vertical aspiration.
'Edifice' maps a stable, load-bearing architectural structure onto the constitutional-political order, implying it has foundations, walls, and the possibility of structural decay or violation.
'Transmit' maps the physical handover of a durable object across generations (inheritance frame) onto the civic obligation of preservation.
'Unprofaned' imports a ritual-religious register: the edifice is also a sacred space whose sanctity can be violated by an invader's trespass.
'Undecayed' maps organic or material deterioration onto institutional corruption or neglect over time.
'Untorn' maps physical rending or demolition onto usurpation—illegitimate seizure of power that structurally damages the edifice.
The triad of threats (invasion, time-decay, usurpation) corresponds structurally to threats any physical building faces: external assault, material entropy, and internal sabotage.
The metaphor places the current generation in a passive-custodial rather than creative role: their duty is conservation, not construction.
issue147-openai-cmt-final2-20260627: Founders are builders who raise a durable political structure on the land.
Liberty and equal rights form a constructed inheritance that can be handed down.
Political institutions can decay, be torn, or be profaned like material or sacred objects.
The present generation's duty is preservation rather than original construction.

reference_value: building political institutions of liberty and rights
preserving and handing on political liberties and institutions
Institutions are understood through built structure.

issue147-anthropic-cmt-final2-20260627: 'Endure evils' maps the physical capacity to sustain pain, hardship, or pressure onto the civic-psychological capacity to tolerate governmental dysfunction without rebellion.
The body is implicitly the bearer of governmental burdens; political suffering is mapped onto somatic suffering.
'Long and patiently' extends the temporal dimension of physical endurance, implying a threshold beyond which the body (polity) cannot continue to bear the load.
The frame implies a limit: endurance is finite, and once exceeded, breakdown (alienation of affections, change of government) follows necessarily—as a body collapses under excessive weight.
Paired with 'suffer much' earlier in the sentence, 'endure' reinforces a sustained GOVERNMENT AS BURDEN / ORDEAL mapping across the clause complex.

issue147-anthropic-cmt-final2-20260627: 'The nation's wounds' maps physical trauma (lacerated tissue) onto the social, political, and moral damage inflicted by civil war, making the nation a body capable of being hurt.

'Bind up' maps the clinical act of bandaging or suturing a wound onto the political-legislative work of reconstruction and reconciliation, implying that healing requires active skilled intervention, not passive waiting.
The medical frame imports a healer agent (the government, the people collectively) whose moral obligation is care and restoration of the injured body.
Wounds that are bound up are expected to heal and close: the metaphor implies the possibility, indeed the expectation, of full national restoration.

'Cherish a just and lasting peace' extends the caring register: 'cherish' maps the affective nurturing of a fragile or precious living thing (perhaps an infant or a tender plant) onto the cultivation of post-war peace.

The convergence of wound-care and cherishing maps medical and parental-nurturing frames simultaneously onto the post-war political task, constructing reconciliation as both clinical and affective work.

The frame implicitly constructs the Confederacy / the South as a wounded part of the same national body, not an external enemy—a move with significant political and moral entailments for Reconstruction.
issue147-openai-cmt-final2-20260627: The nation is construed as an injured body requiring care after battle.
Political action after war is analogous to dressing wounds and promoting recovery.
Peace is fragile and must be actively cherished or nursed into durability.
Malice would aggravate injury, while charity and care support healing.

reference_value: peace is treated as something to nurture and hold dear
Political reconciliation is understood through healing an injured body.
